# The Collison Brothers: The Story Behind The Founding Of Stripe

[![The Startup Grind Team](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*QBk5xL_cp0Q0G71Jj-x8uw.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@startupgrind?source=post_page---byline--ae013434c080---------------------------------------)

[The Startup Grind Team](https://medium.com/@startupgrind?source=post_page---byline--ae013434c080---------------------------------------)

Follow

8 min read

·

Jun 14, 2016

70

3

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dae013434c080&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fstartup-grind%2Fthe-collison-brothers-the-story-behind-the-founding-of-stripe-ae013434c080&source=---header_actions--ae013434c080---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1280/1*tSU2pJRS444z__XETCYxCg.jpeg)

In 2012, we updated this piece to reflect [Bloomberg’s latest report](http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-02-09/stripe-said-to-get-funding-valuing-online-payment-startup-at-100-million.html) that Stripe had just raised an $18MM round with Sequoia at a $100MM valuation.

On July 28th, 2015 Stripe announced even bigger news: [a strategic partnership and investment from Visa](http://investor.visa.com/news/news-details/2015/Visa-and-Stripe-Partner-to-Expand-Online-Commerce-Globally/default.aspx) that will help Visa provide a better online and mobile experiences, while Stripe will benefit from Visa’s technical tools, like tokenization used to anonimize user transactions, and global reach.

_“Stripe aims to give developers the tools they need to create the most secure and novel buying experiences,” said Patrick Collison, CEO and co-founder of Stripe. “Our partnership with Visa will accelerate our ability to expand to markets around the world, and give developers even more control over the end-to-end experience. We’re very excited about the potential._

But this adventure has humble beginnings. Read on for Startup Grind’s original Stripe founding story, with your founders and protagonists, Patrick and John Collison, and check out our interviews with Patrick Collison over three years of building his company Stripe.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:620/0*ntVBjbcs0bK4xtqw.jpg)

Don’t they look young?

### Beginning the Entrepreneurial Journey

In 2005 at the age of 16, [Patrick Collison](http://commonred.com/project/twitter/patrickc) was the recipient of the 41st Young Scientist of the Year for his work with [Lisp](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lisp_(programming_language)). His brother and co-founder John Collison scored the highest-ever score received by a student for the Irish [Leaving Certificate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leaving_Certificate). Before Patrick’s last year of High School he left early to attend MIT.

During his first quarter of freshman year, Patrick founded Auctomatic and joined Y Combinator as a Winter 2007 company. Its path would not last long. At the age of 19 and ten months after incorporating, [the company was bought](http://techcrunch.com/2008/03/26/communicate-acquires-y-combinator-startup-auctomatic-unveils-new-business-strategy) by Live Current Media for $5MM where Patrick became the Director of Product Engineering in 2008. Meanwhile younger brother John attended Harvard the fall of 2009.

In early 2010 John and Patrick began working on Stripe together. At the time Patrick was working on several side projects and they debated why it was so difficult to accept payments on the web. They sought to solve the problem and see if it was possible to make it simple — really simple. The next 6-months they played with it, showed it to friends, and saw how people interacted with it, iterating along the way.

Within 2-weeks of building the prototype they had their first transactions with a Y Combinator company called 280 North. Eventually its founder [Ross Boucher](http://rossboucher.com/) (their first customer) would join Stripe as one of the first employees.

In the beginning they weren’t sure how big the market was or if they could provide the user experience that they wanted. They also didn’t have the answers to whether or not they could fully address issues like fraud, non-US payments, and solving similar problems that Paypal does but in user-friendly way.

Stripe originally partnered with a payments company but after they started taking it seriously they realized that the only way to control the entire experience the way they wanted to was to control all aspects of the process. They brought everything in house.

After 6-months they determined they were onto something big and that they could create the user experience that they wanted. Both John and Patrick started working on it full-time in the fall of 2010. The company was still bootstrapped, but the founders were starting to realize that as a payment startup they would need institutional credibility that an investor could provide. According to them they “didn’t look great on paper.” (To each his own standards but in my book they qualified as extremely legit. Note first two paragraphs above) John became the President and Patrick the CEO.

### Growing Stripe through Word of Mouth

The team’s friends in turn invited their friends. _“Initially it very much spread through a word of mouth process. That was surprising to us because it’s a payment system not a social network so it’s not something you’d think would have any virality whatsoever. But it became clear that everything else was so bad and so painful to work with that people actually were selling this to their friends,”_ Patrick [recently said](http://techzinglive.com/page/939/168-tz-interview-patrick-collison-stripe) in a TechZing interview.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:300/0*NNBbLm3COpbu1Cae.png)

_Charts courtesy of Compete and Alexa. Translation: they are likely completely inaccurate._

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:300/0*HFwNDrC-yopuUYZc.png)

### Funding FinTech at Y Combinator

Their first funding came from [Y Combinator](https://www.startupgrind.com/blog/sam-altman-on-defining-startup-success/). Patrick knew [Paul Graham](http://commonred.com/project/twitter/paulg) through his involvement with Lisp and Auctomatic, and once they told him about what they were doing, he offered to fund it. Considering he made hundreds of thousands of dollars on Patrick’s previous company, it had to be a no brainer. His Auctomatic co-founder is also YC Partner Harj Taggar.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:594/0*PFyEl_v3Ht87cIwP.jpg)

What’s interesting is that unlike 99% of YC companies, Stripe didn’t actually go through the standard startup bootcamp process. Patrick had already been through it once and didn’t feel the need to do it again. The amount YC invested was in the $20k-$30k range. That next summer they met with [Peter Thiel](https://www.startupgrind.com/blog/peter-thiel-keeping-it-real-about-education-on-60-minutes/) after he spoke at a YC dinner, and the founder of PayPal offered his insights into the Payments market and his learnings from PayPal. After their meeting he offered to invest. [They raised $2MM](http://techcrunch.com/2011/03/28/stealth-payment-startup-stripe-paypal) and added other usual Silicon Valley suspects like Michael Moritz and [Sequoia Capital](https://www.startupgrind.com/events/details/startup-grind-san-francisco-hosted-alfred-lin-sequoia-capital), [Andreessen Horowitz](https://www.startupgrind.com/blog/vc-corner-ben-horowitz-of-andreessen-horowitz-genius-transferwise-foursquare/), and SV Angel.

## Get The Startup Grind Team’s stories in your inbox

Remember me for faster sign in

_“Elon and Peter have been very insightful,”_ Patrick says. _“They have sharp opinions on what PayPal did right and wrong. They have deep appreciation for how difficult it is to get there. They fought this battle for many years and it’s really helpful to have someone who can talk about the future in 15-years, but also someone who can empathize and sympathize with the day-to-day considerations. I don’t think there are that many people who can straddle both sides of that.”_

### Company and Domain Name

When they first started working on the site it was called _/Dev/Payments_. As you can imagine it caused some problems. _“(The name) is horrendous to say. Plus most schemas don’t allow slashes in company names. So we decided we needed to change it.”_

Names like PayStack and PayDemon were on the list but eventually they started to look any names they could think of and seeing what was present on the domain. [Stripe.com](http://stripe.com/) had a lukewarm reception at first, but over time is seemed to have emotion and importantly was not associated with any other specific company or brand.

The domain owner was an MIT alumni and the subsequent sale for tens of thousands of dollars was relatively smooth. In June of 2011, roughly 18-months after the company began, the first designer joined the team and synced the brand and messaging which lives throughout their product and website.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:300/0*32SIgu6M_wQiQ0bR.png)

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:300/0*IoRPCA8WTaW_tDsP.png)

The company’s office is in downtown Palo Alto and at time of writing, they had 17 total employees. Their hiring litmus test is that they’ll hire you as long as you’re someone the team would want to come into the office on Saturday, just to hang out with you. If not then they’re not getting the job.

### Security, 7-Day Waiting Period, Costs

While their merchant approval process is instant, Stripe has a mandatory 7-day waiting period for each transaction before you can get your money. The founders say that’s the only way to do the fast and simple setup.

_“Because it’s seven day that gives us time to really get an accurate picture as to the profile of the business. And because Stripe provides the easiest setup process of any payment system on the internet, that creates an attractive fraud target. We feel confident in our system and ability to detect that, but we can’t do it overnight.”_

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*8b-4KY3GXVuTRyAP.png)

_“It has taken a ton of work and there’s a good reason that no one has done this before. But it’s also the one part of the product we’re unwilling to compromise. We always wanted a payment system that we could just go live with immediately and not have to fax documents or wait till the next day or call us.”_

At 2.9% (plus $0.30 per transaction) Stripe is hardly the cheapest solution to process a credit card. But Stripe’s argument is that when you add up the monthly fees, setup fees, gateway fees, PCI fees, International Credit Card fees, and others you’re actually paying a lot more than you think. For instance if you try to process a card unsuccessfully then other providers will charge you a fee for the unsuccessful attempt. _“We wanted to make sure that we only make money if you make money. It’s a messaging challenge for us to explain to people that they’re paying more for their credit card processing then they think,”_ Collison said.

I’m excited to see what they guys accomplish and how they grow. There’s a lot of interesting startup competition in the space like [Paypal haters WePay](https://www.startupgrind.com/events/details/startup-grind-silicon-valley-hosted-bill-clerico-wepay), Square, Dwolla, and even [Gumroad](http://startupgrind.com/2012/01/gumroad-is-marking-selling-anything-dead-simple/). Clearly Stripe is hitting a chord with developers and other segments desperate for a better solution. When they release their traffic and dollars processed totals, people are going to be blown away.

_Catch the full 2012 interview below, or see_ [_all our live sessions with Patrick Collison of Stripe over 3 years_](https://www.startupgrind.com/blog/patrick-collison-founder-stripe-live-at-startup-grind-video/) _._

International"),[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-9) and venture capital firms [Sequoia Capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequoia_Capital "Sequoia Capital"), [Andreessen Horowitz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andreessen_Horowitz "Andreessen Horowitz"), and [SV Angel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SV_Angel "SV Angel").[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-telegraph-20210223-10) In March 2013, Stripe made its first [acquisition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Takeover "Takeover"), Kickoff, a chat and [task-management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Task_management "Task management") application.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-11) In 2012 the company moved from Palo Alto to San Francisco.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:2-12) In October 2019, the company announced that it would be moving from the [South of Market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_of_Market "South of Market") area to [Oyster Point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oyster_Point_Marina/Park "Oyster Point Marina/Park") in the neighbouring city of [South San Francisco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_San_Francisco "South San Francisco") in 2021.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:2-12) In February 2021, [Mark Carney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark_Carney "Mark Carney"), former [governor of the Bank of Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_Canada "Governor of the Bank of Canada") and of the [Bank of England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_England "Governor of the Bank of England"), was appointed to the company's board.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-telegraph-20210223-10) Carney stepped down from his role with the company in 2025 in order to run for the leadership of the [Liberal Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada").[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-13) Stripe acquired accountancy platform Recko in October 2021 whose solution was to be added to Stripe's existing suite of financial tools.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-14) In January 2022, Stripe entered a five-year partnership with [Ford Motor Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"). Through the deal, Stripe would handle transactions for consumer vehicle orders and reservations.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-15) That same month, Stripe partnered with [Spotify](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") to help the company monetize subscriptions.[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-16) In April 2022, Twitter announced that it would partner with Stripe, Inc. (digital payments processor) for piloting cryptocurrency pay-outs for limited users in the platform.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-17) In April 2022, Stripe announced its strategic partnership with UK-based financial technology company ION.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-18) The _Wall Street Journal_ reported in July 2022 that the company's internal share price had fallen, causing its implied valuation to drop from $95 billion to $74 billion.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-19) In November 2022, the company announced it intended to initiate layoffs, terminating some 14% of its workforce.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-20) Throughout 2022 and 2023, the company announced a number of large enterprise customers, including Airbnb,[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-21) Amazon,[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-22) Microsoft,[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-23) Uber,[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-24) BMW,[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-25) Maersk,[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-26) Zara,[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-27) Lotus,[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-28) Alaska Airlines,[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-29) Le Monde,[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-30) and Toyota.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-31) The company also announced in March 2023 that OpenAI is working with Stripe to commercialize its generative AI technology.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-32) In January 2025, Stripe sent layoff notices to nearly 300 workers, primarily affecting roles in Product, Operations and Engineering. The company experienced controversy when the company sent a cartoon picture of a duck to the laid-off employees. Stripe's Chief People Officer Rob McIntosh later apologized for the mistake.[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-33) After re-enabling cryptocurrency pay-ins in April 2024, starting with [USDC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USDC_(cryptocurrency) "USDC (cryptocurrency)"), Stripe completed the acquisition of Bridge in February 2025.[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-TCCrypto2024-34) The acquisition of the two-year-old [stablecoin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stablecoin "Stablecoin") platform company is valued at $1.1 billion.[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-35)[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-36) In June 2025, the company acquired Privy, which powers crypto wallets.[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-37) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Stripe_Dublin_office.jpg/250px-Stripe_Dublin_office.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Stripe_Dublin_office.jpg) Dublin headquarters In September 2025, Stripe announced it was powering Instant Checkout in [ChatGPT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT") and released Agentic Commerce Protocol for [agentic commerce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agentic_commerce "Agentic commerce"), which was co-developed with [OpenAI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenAI "OpenAI").[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-38) In October 2025, the company opened its second headquarters in Dublin, Ireland.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-39) In February 2026, Stripe was valued at $159 billion in a tender offer posted for employees and shareholders. The tender offer was about a 70% increase from Stripe's previous valuation published in February 2025, where it was valued at $91.5 billion. Stripe also announced that its total volume increased to $1.9 trillion USD in 2025, a 34% increase from 2024.[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:5-40)[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-41) ## Technology company \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=2 "Edit section: Technology company")\] ### Payment processing \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=3 "Edit section: Payment processing")\] Stripe provides [application programming interfaces](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Application_programming_interfaces "Application programming interfaces") that [web developers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_developers "Web developers") can use to integrate [payment processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Payment_processing "Payment processing") into their websites and mobile applications.[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-42) The company introduced Stripe Connect in 2012, a multiparty payments solution that lets software developers embed payments natively into their products.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-43) In April 2018, Stripe released antifraud tools, branded "Radar", that block fraudulent transactions.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-44) The same year, it expanded its services to include a billing product for online businesses, allowing businesses to manage subscription [recurring revenue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recurring_revenue "Recurring revenue") and [invoicing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Invoicing "Invoicing").[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-45) Stripe's [point-of-sale](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Point-of-sale "Point-of-sale") service called Terminal was made available to US users on 11 June 2019. Terminal had previously been invitation-only.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-46) Terminal is currently available in Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Ireland, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Singapore, and the United Kingdom. The service offers physical credit-card [readers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Payment_terminal "Payment terminal") designed to work with Stripe.[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-47)[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-48) On 5 September 2019, Stripe launched a merchant cash-advance scheme called Stripe Capital. The scheme allows Stripe merchants to request an advance on future payments they expect to process through their Stripe merchant account.[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-49) In June 2021, the company launched Stripe Tax, a service to allow businesses to automatically calculate and collect sales tax, VAT, and GST, initially rolling out to 30 countries and all US states.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-50) As of 2025, it has been made available in 102 countries.[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-tcaiannounce-51) In May that year, Stripe introduced Payment Links, a no-code product allowing businesses to create a link to a checkout page and begin accepting payments on social platforms or direct channels.[\[52\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-52) In January 2022, Stripe agreed to acquire Terminal manufacturing partner BBPOS, allowing the company to bring the hardware development of Terminal readers in-house.[\[53\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-53) In February, it was announced as Apple's first partner on in-person Tap to Pay, which enables businesses to accept contactless payments using an iPhone and a partner-enabled iOS app.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-54) In May, Stripe announced Data Pipeline, a tool for Stripe users who store data with Amazon Redshift or Snowflake Data Cloud. Data Pipeline syncs Stripe data and reports with Amazon Redshift or Snowflake Data Cloud, where they can be queried in combination with other business information.[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-55) That month, the company also introduced Stripe Financial Connections, enabling businesses to establish direct connections with their customers’ bank accounts to verify accounts for payments and pay-outs, check balances to reduce payment failures, and cut fraud by confirming bank account ownership.[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-56) In September 2023, Stripe announced that its optimized checkout suite allowed businesses to offer their customers more than 100 payment methods.[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-pymnts.com-57) In May 2025, Stripe announced a new AI foundational model for payments, and introduced [stablecoin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stablecoin "Stablecoin") powered accounts.[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-tcaiannounce-51) ### Corporate finance \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=4 "Edit section: Corporate finance")\] In July 2018, Stripe introduced Stripe Issuing, a product that allows online businesses and platforms to create their own physical and digital credit and debit cards.[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-58)[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-59) ### Atlas \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=5 "Edit section: Atlas")\] On 14 February 2016, the company launched the Atlas platform to help start-ups register as US corporations, targeting foreign entrepreneurs.[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:4-60) The platform was originally invitation-only.[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-61) In March 2016, [Cuba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") was added to the list of countries covered under the program.[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-62) Originally, companies registered using Atlas were set up as Delaware-based [C corporations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C_corporation "C corporation"). As of 30 April 2018, the option to be registered as [limited liability companies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Limited_liability_companies "Limited liability companies") was added.[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-63) Companies set up using Atlas automatically had a business bank account and Stripe merchant account set up.[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:4-60) ### Link \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=6 "Edit section: Link")\] In May 2021, Stripe launched Link,[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:3-64) a service for saving and auto-filling payment details when paying via Stripe.[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-65)[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-66) The service supported payments in over 185 countries and Stripe reported plans to make it available to platform businesses through its [API](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/API "API").[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:3-64) In September 2025, Patrick Collison announced that Link had surpassed 200 million users.[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-67) ### Other \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=7 "Edit section: Other")\] In 2018, Stripe started a publishing company named Stripe Press to promote ideas that support businesses.[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-68)[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-69) In 2019, Stripe began offering [loans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loan "Loan") and credit cards to businesses in the United States. The company stated that loans are approved automatically using machine-learning models, with no human intervention.[\[70\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-70) The following year, the company introduced Stripe Treasury, which provides its platform users APIs to embed financial services, allowing their customers to send, receive, and store funds.[\[71\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-71) In October 2020, Stripe announced Stripe Climate, a service for businesses to fund atmospheric carbon [research and capture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_capture_and_storage "Carbon capture and storage").[\[72\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-72) In 2022, Stripe started a new subsidiary called Frontier that would direct spending on [carbon removal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_removal "Carbon removal"). It announced $925million in funding from major Silicon Valley companies to fund start up companies performing carbon capture to kick-start the industry.[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-73) Stripe Identity, launched in June 2021, enables online businesses to verify user identities and is built on the same infrastructure used for Stripe's own risk and compliance program.[\[74\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-74) In May 2022, Stripe launched Stripe Apps to allow businesses to simplify operations and combine fragmented workflows.[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-pymnts.com-57) Stripe also announced Financial Connections to help businesses connect to their customers’ bank accounts for verifying bank accounts, checking balances, and confirming account ownership.[\[75\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-75) ## Stripe App Marketplace \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=8 "Edit section: Stripe App Marketplace")\] In May 2022, Stripe announced the launch of an [App Marketplace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/App_store "App store") allowing users to customize Stripe with third-party app integrations.[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-76) At launch, the Marketplace had over 50 apps including offerings from [DocuSign](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DocuSign "DocuSign"), [Dropbox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dropbox "Dropbox"), [Intercom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercom,_Inc. "Intercom, Inc."), [Mailchimp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mailchimp "Mailchimp"), [Ramp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramp_(company) "Ramp (company)"), and [Xero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xero_(company) "Xero (company)").[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-77) While the marketplace was launched in May, app installations were not available immediately.[\[78\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-78) The move followed Stripe's acquisition of OpenChannel, a company which built app ecosystems for businesses, in December 2021.[\[79\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-79) ## Growth \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=9 "Edit section: Growth")\] In May 2011, Stripe received a $2million investment from venture capitalists [Peter Thiel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Thiel "Peter Thiel"), [Elon Musk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), [Sequoia Capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequoia_Capital "Sequoia Capital"), [SV Angel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SV_Angel "SV Angel"), and [Andreessen Horowitz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andreessen_Horowitz "Andreessen Horowitz").[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-80) Stripe launched publicly in September 2011 after an extensive private [beta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_test "Beta test").[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-81) In 2020 Stripe expanded its services to five new European markets: the Czech Republic, Romania, Bulgaria, Cyprus, and Malta.[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-82) On 15 October 2020, Stripe acquired [Paystack](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paystack "Paystack"), a [Nigerian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") payment processor, in a deal reportedly worth over $200million, with the aim of expanding its services into Africa.[\[83\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-83) In December 2020, Stripe announced plans to expand in Southeast Asia, China, India, and Japan. It increased its staff in the region by 200 employees.[\[84\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-84) In March 2021, Stripe raised another $600million, reaching a valuation of $95billion, aimed towards expanding its European headquarters.[\[85\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-TechCrunch-85) In April 2021, Stripe acquired TaxJar, a provider of cloud-based tax services based in Massachusetts. Whilst details of the acquisition were not made public, the deal is thought to have been in the region of $200M.[\[86\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-86) In March 2023, Stripe completed its Series I fundraise of more than $6.5 billion at a $50 billion valuation. The funds raised will be used to provide liquidity to current and former employees and address employee withholding tax obligations related to equity awards. The company has said it does not need this capital to run its business.[\[87\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-87) On 3 December 2025, Stripe acquired the usage-based billing platform Metronome.[\[88\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-88) ## Investments \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=10 "Edit section: Investments")\] Stripe is reported to have participated in two funding rounds for [Monzo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monzo "Monzo"), a " [challenger bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Challenger_bank "Challenger bank")" based in the U.K. Stripe's first investment in Monzo was reported on 6 November 2017,[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:0-89) with a second investment in Monzo's Series E fundraising round reported on 10 October 2018.[\[90\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:1-90) Monzo's valuation grew from approximately $350million[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:0-89) to $1.27billion[\[90\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-:1-90) through these two rounds of fundraising. Stripe participated in a third round of funding for Monzo on 24 June 2019, which raised approximately $144million in funding for Monzo at a valuation of approximately $2.5billion.[\[91\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-91)[\[92\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-92) Stripe has invested in companies offering similar services as themselves, but in different geographical regions. In August 2018, Stripe invested in [PayStack](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PayStack "PayStack"), a Nigerian payment processor,[\[93\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-93) and, in September 2019, invested in PayMongo, a [Philippine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippine "Philippine") payment processor.[\[94\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-94) In February 2021, Stripe invested in Safepay, a Pakistani payment processor.[\[95\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-95) On 6 June 2019, Stripe led a $22.5million fundraising round for Step, a financial services start-up offering fee-free bank accounts to teenagers.[\[96\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-96) On 26 March 2020, Stripe led a $20million Series A fundraising round for Fast, a company creating a universal, one-click checkout service.[\[97\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-97) Subsequently, Stripe led a $102million Series B fundraising round for Fast on January 26, 2021.[\[98\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-98) Fast shut down in April 2022.[\[99\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-99) On 3 November 2021, Stripe led a $4million Series Seed fundraising round for Archive, a social listening and user-generated content platform.[\[100\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-100) In April 2022, Stripe Inc., [Alphabet Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc."), [Shopify](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shopify "Shopify"), [McKinsey & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company"), and [Meta Platforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms") announced Frontier, a $925million [advance market commitment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advance_market_commitment "Advance market commitment") of [carbon dioxide removal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide_removal "Carbon dioxide removal") (CDR) from companies that are developing CDR technology over the next 9 years.[\[101\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-101)[\[102\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-102) On 15 July 2024, it was reported that major Stripe investor [Sequoia Capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequoia_Capital "Sequoia Capital") offered its limited partners a chance to sell up to $861million worth of shares in Stripe. This move, indicating confidence in Stripe's future despite the delayed [IPO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IPO "IPO"), was driven by Sequoia's efforts to provide liquidity to its investors amid a dry IPO market. Stripe's latest 409A valuation stood at $70billion, solidifying its position as one of the most valued start-ups globally.[\[103\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-103)[\[104\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-104) On 26 July 2024, Stripe acquired a Merchant of Record services start-up Lemon Squeezy.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-105)[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-106) ## Litigation \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=11 "Edit section: Litigation")\] In March 2026, the [Federal Trade Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") issued a warning against Stripe and three other payment processing brands for debanking of law-abiding citizens over political, religious or certain law-abiding activities.[\[107\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_note-107) ## See also \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=12 "Edit section: See also")\] - [Electronic commerce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electronic_commerce "Electronic commerce") - [List of online payment service providers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_online_payment_service_providers "List of online payment service providers") - [Payment gateway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Payment_gateway "Payment gateway") - [Payment service provider](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Payment_service_provider "Payment service provider") - [Subscription business model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subscription_business_model "Subscription business model") ## References \[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Stripe,_Inc.&action=edit§ion=13 "Edit section: References")\] 001. **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stripe,_Inc.#cite_ref-weinberg_1-0 "Jump up")**Weinberg, Cory (26 March 2025). From the Editor

## Last Updated Mar 10, 2026, 1:01am EDT

- Patrick Collison is the cofounder and CEO of Stripe, a company that lets businesses and individuals accept payments over the internet.
- Private investors valued Stripe at $159 billion in February 2026.
- Patrick and his brother John hatched the idea for Stripe while both were in college in Cambridge at MIT.
- Patrick handles engineering and serves as the public face of the company.
- An avid reader, Collison keeps hundreds of books on his shelves on topics including physics, feminism and literary criticism.

Privacy preferences

We use cookies and similar technologies to provide you with the best possible experience on amnistreasury.com. Some are essential for this website to function; others help us understand how you use this site, so we can improve it. We may also use targeted cookies for advertising purpose. Click "Accept all cookies" to continue as specified, or "Manage my preferences" to select the type of cookies you would like to accept.

Some services process personal data in unsecure third countries. By consenting to the use of these services, you also consent to the processing of your data in these unsecure third countries in accordance with Art. 49 (1) (a) GDPR. This involves risks that your data will be processed by authorities for control and monitoring purposes, perhaps without the possibility of a legal recourse.

You also allow data processing in accordance with Google Consent Mode of participating partners on the basis of consent for the following purposes:

1. Sending user data to Google for online advertising purposes.
2. Evaluation and display of personalized advertising.
3. Storing and reading of data such as cookies (web) or device identifiers (apps), related to analytics (e.g. visit duration).
4. Storing and reading of data such as cookies (web) or device identifiers (apps) related to advertising.
5. Storing and reading of data that supports the functionality of the website or app (e.g. language settings).
6. Storing and reading of data related to personalization (e.g. video recommendations).
7. Storing and reading of data related to security (e.g. authentication functionality, fraud prevention, and other user protection).

[Data protection](https://amnistreasury.com/data-protection/)

CustomiseReject AllAccept All

Powered by [Visit CookieYes website](https://www.cookieyes.com/product/cookie-consent/?ref=cypbcyb&utm_source=cookie-banner&utm_medium=fl-branding)

Customise Consent Preferences![cky-close-icon](https://cdn-cookieyes.com/assets/images/close.svg)

We use cookies to help you navigate efficiently and perform certain functions. You will find detailed information about all cookies under each consent category below.

The cookies that are categorised as "Necessary" are stored on your browser as they are essential for enabling the basic functionalities of the site. ... Show more

NecessaryAlways Active

Necessary cookies are required to enable the basic features of this site, such as providing secure log-in or adjusting your consent preferences. These cookies do not store any personally identifiable data.

- Cookie

\_\_cf\_bm

- Duration

1 hour

- Description

This cookie, set by Cloudflare, is used to support Cloudflare Bot Management.


- Cookie

wpml\_browser\_redirect\_test

- Duration

session

- Description



This cookie is set by WPML WordPress plugin and is used to test if cookies are enabled on the browser.


- Cookie

\_icl\_visitor\_lang\_js

- Duration

1 day

- Description



WPML sets this cookie to store the redirected language.


- Cookie

wp-wpml\_current\_language

- Duration

session

- Description



WordPress multilingual plugin sets this cookie to store the current language/language settings.


- Cookie

\_cfuvid

- Duration

session

- Description

Cloudflare sets this cookie to track users across sessions to optimize user experience by maintaining session consistency and providing personalized services


- Cookie

pb-portal-didomi-consent-string-cookie

- Duration

1 year

- Description



Set by Didomi's consent management platform on the ProductBoard ideas portal (ideas.amnistreasury.com) to store the visitor's consent preferences as an encoded string. Required to remember and honor the user's cookie choices across sessions on the ideas portal subdomain.


- Cookie

server

- Duration

session

- Description



Identifies the backend server handling the visitor's session on the developer portal (developer.amnistreasury.com) to ensure consistent routing and session continuity. Required for the portal to function correctly.


- Cookie

rc::a

- Duration

Never Expires

- Description

This cookie is set by the Google recaptcha service to identify bots to protect the website against malicious spam attacks.


- Cookie

rc::c

- Duration

session

- Description

This cookie is set by the Google recaptcha service to identify bots to protect the website against malicious spam attacks.


- Cookie

cookietest

- Duration

session

- Description

The cookietest cookie is typically used to determine whether the user's browser accepts cookies, essential for website functionality and user experience.


- Cookie

cookieyes-\*

- Duration

1 year

- Description

CookieYes sets this cookie for consent solution management.


- Cookie

m

- Duration

1 year 1 month 4 days

- Description

Stripe sets this cookie for fraud prevention purposes. It identifies the device used to access the website, allowing the website to be formatted accordingly.


Functional

Functional cookies help perform certain functionalities like sharing the content of the website on social media platforms, collecting feedback, and other third-party features.

- Cookie

X-CSRF-TOKEN

- Duration

session

- Description

Stores a unique anti-forgery token to validate user-initiated requests, safeguarding forms, APIs, and transactions against cross-site request forgery attack attempts.


- Cookie

li\_gc

- Duration

6 months

- Description

Linkedin set this cookie for storing visitor's consent regarding using cookies for non-essential purposes.


- Cookie

lidc

- Duration

1 day

- Description

LinkedIn sets the lidc cookie to facilitate data center selection.


- Cookie

recent\_write

- Duration

Less than a minute

- Description



Stores a timestamp of the user’s most recent content update to manage state, prevent duplicate submissions, and optimize support interactions.


- Cookie

gtm\_id

- Duration

1 year

- Description



Set by Intercom's internal tag management configuration to identify the visitor and manage messenger widget behavior across sessions. Used to maintain consistent chat experience when navigating between pages.


- Cookie

\_pbd

- Duration

14 days

- Description



Stores session and form state for roadmap idea submissions, enabling authentication, retaining details, restoring progress, preventing duplicates, and mitigating spam.


- Cookie

ljs-lang

- Duration

Never Expires

- Description



Stores the visitor's language preference on the application subdomain (app.amnistreasury.com) to maintain consistent localisation of the user interface across sessions and page loads.


- Cookie

intercom-id-\*

- Duration

8 months 26 days 1 hour

- Description



Intercom sets this cookie that allows visitors to see any conversations they've had on Intercom websites.


- Cookie

intercom-session-\*

- Duration

7 days

- Description



Intercom sets this cookie that allows visitors to see any conversations they've had on Intercom websites.


- Cookie

intercom-device-id-\*

- Duration

8 months 26 days 1 hour

- Description



Intercom sets this cookie that allows visitors to see any conversations they've had on Intercom websites.


- Cookie

referrer\_user\_id

- Duration

14 days

- Description

Calendly sets this cookie for the booking functionality of the website.


- Cookie

\_calendly\_session

- Duration

21 days

- Description



Calendly, a Meeting Schedulers, sets this cookie to allow the meeting scheduler to function within the website and to add events into the visitor’s calendar.


- Cookie

sp\_t

- Duration

1 year

- Description

The sp\_t cookie is set by Spotify to implement audio content from Spotify on the website and also registers information on user interaction related to the audio content.


- Cookie

sp\_landing

- Duration

1 day

- Description

The sp\_landing is set by Spotify to implement audio content from Spotify on the website and also registers information on user interaction related to the audio content.


Analytics

Analytical cookies are used to understand how visitors interact with the website. These cookies help provide information on metrics such as the number of visitors, bounce rate, traffic source, etc.

- Cookie

nabAlternative

- Duration

We use Amplitude to understand how the amnis webapp is used — which features you visit, where users encounter friction, and which improvements to prioritise. Amplitude does not receive your payment instructions, account numbers, beneficiary details or any other transactional content. The data is stored on European Union infrastructure. You can withdraw your consent at any time in this privacy settings panel.



**Purposes:** Product analytics, user-experience improvement, feature optimisation. AI-enabled internal analytical exploration of the pseudonymised dataset (see separate toggle below).



**Categories of data processed**



  - page-view and click events within the webapp

**What is stored on your device:** a device / installation identifier and a session identifier in local storage. No third-party tracking cookies are set by amnis for this purpose.

**Where the data is processed:** European Union (Amazon Web Services, Frankfurt region).

**Third-country transfers:** limited residual transfers to the United States and to a small number of other locations (operational logging, AI sub-processors, remote technical support). These transfers are protected by the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (including its Swiss-US Extension and UK Extension) and, where the Framework does not apply, by the EU 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses.

**Retention:** 1 year from collection (planned to extend to 2 years on the next subscription upgrade; this notice will be updated at that point).

**Legal basis:** Your consent under Article 6(1)(a) GDPR and Article 5(3) ePrivacy Directive (and equivalent national implementations including Liechtenstein TKG and Swiss FADP). For the server-side analysis of the data already collected, amnis' legitimate interest under Article 6(1)(f) GDPR in maintaining and improving the webapp.

**How to withdraw consent:** toggle this entry off here at any time. Withdrawal does not affect the lawfulness of processing carried out before withdrawal.

**Provider's privacy notice:** [https://amplitude.com/privacy](https://amplitude.com/privacy)

**Provider's sub-processor list:** [https://www.amplitude.com/subprocessor-list](https://www.amplitude.com/subprocessor-list)

# Alternatives to Stripe for businesses in Switzerland

![Illustration of a woman managing international payments on a computer, with the text “Best Stripe alternatives in Switzerland” and icons representing different currencies.](https://amnistreasury.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/Stripe-alternatives.png)

- International payments
- Money transfer

04.11.2025

![Elena Tankovski](https://amnistreasury.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/elena-square-concept-hi-res-01-00-50x50.png)

Elena Tankovski

[Share](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/alternatives-to-stripe-for-businesses-in-switzerland/# "Facebook")[Share](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/alternatives-to-stripe-for-businesses-in-switzerland/# "Twitter")[Share](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/alternatives-to-stripe-for-businesses-in-switzerland/# "Linkedin")

With its powerful tools and checkout solutions, Stripe has become a leading payment platform for online businesses worldwide. While it is widely used by e-commerce companies and digital platforms, businesses today look for complementary or alternative providers that better match their specific needs. Especially when it comes to multi-currency accounts, cross-border payments, debit cards or tailored SME solutions, several other options exist that can serve as strong alternatives to Stripe. This article explores the best providers similar to Stripe in Switzerland.

## What is Stripe known for?

Stripe is primarily designed for e-commerce platforms and online marketplaces, as it provides developers and businesses with easy-to-integrate payment solutions. Its core strengths lie in its robust API, checkout experience, and wide compatibility with online platforms.

With Stripe, businesses can accept payments in multiple currencies, manage subscriptions, and automate billing. Thanks to its extensive integrations, Stripe has become a preferred solution for many online businesses worldwide, especially those looking for a robust digital payment infrastructure.

## Why do companies look for a Stripe alternative in 2025?

While Stripe remains popular, certain drawbacks make it less attractive for internationally active Swiss businesses.

- **Payout restrictions**: Local payment schemes only available in select regions
- **High cross-border fees**: 1.50 USD, 1.50 GBP, or 1.50 EUR per payout, plus 0.25–1.25% of the transaction amount
- **High FX fees**: +1% for US senders or 2% for non-US senders

For Swiss SMEs, these costs and restrictions quickly add up, which makes a possible Stripe alternative with better multi-currency management and advanced cross-border capabilities more appealing.

## Top Stripe alternatives

Several providers similar to Stripe serve the Swiss market with tailored solutions for international payments and multi-currency management. The following sections give a comparison across key features.

|  | amnis | Stripe | Wise | PayPal | Payoneer |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Main target group | Internationally active SMEs | eCommerce & online businesses | Freelancers & SMEs | Individuals, eCommerce, enterprises | Freelancers, marketplaces, eCommerce |
| **Multi-currency features** |
| Multi-currency account | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Multi-currency debit cards | Yes | No | Yes (additional 4 CHF per each card) | Limited | Limited, additional fees for first card |
| Recurring currency exchange | Yes | N/A | Yes | N/A | N/A |
| FX fees | Starting from mid-rate +0.20% | +1% for US senders or 2% for non-US senders | Depending on region, amount and currency | Up to interbank rate +4% | Interbank rate +0.5% |
| **International Transactions** |
| Access to local payment schemes | Yes | Limited | Yes | No | Limited |
| SWIFT payments | Yes | No | Yes | Limited | Yes |
| Global bulk payments | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| P2P payments | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Currency Hedging** |
| Spot transactions | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Limit orders | Yes | No | Yes | No | No |
| Forward contracts | Yes | No | N/A | No | No |
| Foreign exchange rate alert | Yes | No | Yes | N/A | N/A |
| **Integrations** |
| 3rd party integrations | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| API | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

### amnis

amnis is a Swiss-based fintech designed specifically for SMEs managing international payments. Unlike Stripe, amnis focuses on cross-border efficiency with its [multi-currency account](https://amnistreasury.com/multi-currency-business-account/), [multi-currency debit cards](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/easier-payments-with-multi-currency-debit-card/), and advanced hedging options such as forward contracts and limit orders. It offers direct access to local payment schemes with low FX fees, which makes it especially attractive for businesses with frequent cross-border transactions.

Even better: amnis’ [virtual IBAN accounts](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/virtual-iban-accounts-for-businesses/) in multiple currencies, along with US and Canadian account numbers, can be added to Stripe just like a regular bank account – an ideal solution to avoid costly Stripe conversion fees and to [reduce exchange rate risk](https://amnistreasury.com/blog/how-to-mitigate-exchange-rate-risk-and-fluctuation/) without opening foreign currency accounts at your local bank.

### Stripe

With approximately 1.35 million active websites in 2025, Stripe is one of the most established players in the digital payments space and has achieved strong market penetration worldwide. Stripe has become the go-to solution for many e-commerce platforms and online businesses, as its ecosystem of tools supports everything from subscription billing to marketplace payments.

Over the years, Stripe has built a reputation as a highly adaptable payments provider, which offers businesses a globally recognised and trusted solution to manage online transactions.

### Wise

Wise is a popular choice for freelancers and SMEs with frequent international payments. Its strength lies in its transparent and low-cost FX structure. Wise multi-currency accounts and debit cards are widely used to hold, convert, and spend in different currencies.

The platform supports access to local payment schemes in many countries, which makes cross-border payments faster and cheaper. Additionally, Wise offers a user-friendly interface and a powerful mobile app with a flat learning curve.

### PayPal

With over 30 million global merchants, PayPal is one of the most recognised and trusted online payment solutions worldwide. It is especially popular among eCommerce platforms and online sellers thanks to its fast setup, ease of use, and extensive customer base. PayPal supports multi-currency accounts and offers debit cards, which makes it easy for businesses to accept and manage payments across different markets.

On the other hand, its fees are relatively high compared to other providers. Nevertheless, its extensive global reach and integration with marketplaces make it a convenient, though costly Stripe alternative.

### Payoneer

Payoneer is a leading global payments platform similar to Stripe with a strong adoption in Greater China, Europe, and Asia-Pacific regions. Its merchant services segment has grown substantially, mainly driven by expansion in markets such as China and Vietnam.

Payoneer supports multi-currency accounts, cross-border payments, and debit card solutions to help businesses automate operations, manage global teams, and reach new markets more efficiently.

## amnis or Stripe – Which provider to choose in Switzerland?

For Swiss businesses, Stripe remains a leading choice for e-commerce platforms, while amnis offers efficient multi-currency management, transparent FX fees, advanced treasury tools, and spending solutions like debit cards.

That’s why amnis is a powerful complement to Stripe: With its local CHF, EUR, and GBP IBANs as well as domestic account numbers in USD and CAD, amnis is the ideal solution for receiving foreign currencies, while bypassing Stripe’s high FX fees and the need for foreign bank accounts.

## amnis – The smart add-on to Stripe for Swiss SMEs

Swiss companies using Stripe can now extend their global payment capabilities with amnis – the smart add-on designed for growing and internationally active SMEs. With multi-currency accounts, local IBANs, and transparent FX starting at interbank rates +0.2%, amnis makes cross-border payments effortless and cost-efficient.

The platform connects seamlessly with Stripe and existing finance systems through API and [multiple integrations](https://amnistreasury.com/integrations/), enabling end-to-end automation across payments, expenses, and reporting. Businesses gain real-time control over all accounts and cards in one dashboard – with 24/7 self-service, a free [peer-to-peer network](https://amnistreasury.com/peer-to-peer-payment/), and local Swiss support.

Built on Swiss reliability and European reach, amnis empowers finance teams to save time, reduce costs, and scale globally – without changing their existing banking setup.

[Try for free](https://app.amnistreasury.com/create-an-account?language=en)

![Elena Tankovski](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%2096%2096'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

##### Elena Tankovski

As Senior Content Writer at amnis, Elena transforms complex financial and banking topics into clear, insightful content for SMEs. She focuses on areas such as the FX market, international payments, cross-border business operations, and regulatory updates - ensuring companies have access to reliable and easy-to-understand guidance.

With a strong background in research and communication, Elena plays a key role in helping businesses stay informed, make smarter decisions, and navigate the evolving world of international finance.

# Stripe Competitors: The 12 Best Alternatives to Stripe, Square, PayPal, and other Big Tech Payment Processors

[Subscribe For Free!](https://blog.businessleadershiptoday.com/subscribe/)

Share:

- [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fbusinessleadershiptoday.com%2Fstripe-competitors-alternatives-to-stripe-square-paypal%2F&t=Stripe%20Competitors%3A%20The%2012%20Best%20Alternatives%20to%20Stripe%2C%20Square%2C%20PayPal%2C%20and%20other%20Big%20Tech%20Payment%20Processors "Share on Facebook")
- [Twitter](https://x.com/intent/tweet?text=Stripe%20Competitors%3A%20The%2012%20Best%20Alternatives%20to%20Stripe%2C%20Square%2C%20PayPal%2C%20and%20other%20Big%20Tech%20Payment%20Processors&url=https%3A%2F%2Fbusinessleadershiptoday.com%2Fstripe-competitors-alternatives-to-stripe-square-paypal%2F "Share on X")
- [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fbusinessleadershiptoday.com%2Fstripe-competitors-alternatives-to-stripe-square-paypal%2F&title=Stripe%20Competitors%3A%20The%2012%20Best%20Alternatives%20to%20Stripe%2C%20Square%2C%20PayPal%2C%20and%20other%20Big%20Tech%20Payment%20Processors&summary=The%2012%20best%20alternatives%20to%20Stripe%2C%20Square%2C%20PayPal%2C%20and%20other%20big%20tech%20payment%20processors%20include%3A%20Stax%20Payments%20Ayden%20WePay%20Authorize.net%20Braintree%202Checkout%20Helcim%20Payment%20Depot%20Shopify%20Clover%20ChargeBee%20Wise&mini=true "Share on LinkedIn")
- [Email](mailto:?subject=Stripe%20Competitors%3A%20The%2012%20Best%20Alternatives%20to%20Stripe%2C%20Square%2C%20PayPal%2C%20and%20other%20Big%20Tech%20Payment%20Processors&body=https%3A%2F%2Fbusinessleadershiptoday.com%2Fstripe-competitors-alternatives-to-stripe-square-paypal%2F "Send over email")

[Muriel Call](https://businessleadershiptoday.com/about-business-leadership-today/#our-team "Muriel Call"), Staff Writer

![](https://businessleadershiptoday.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Stripe-competitors.jpg)

Many business leaders and owners assume that credit card and Automated Clearing House (ACH) payment processing fees are simply a non-negotiable part of doing business.

Thus, the first payment processor they select is usually a recognizable name like Stripe, Square, or PayPal.

These well-known companies make it easy to start accepting payments and for customers to pay businesses, especially online.

However, there are many alternatives to Stripe, Square, PayPal, and other big tech payment processors that can offer the same ease of integration and simple user experience while also offering a number of advantages to businesses.

In this article, we’re going to provide you with a detailed analysis of the top Stripe competitors, and the 12 best alternatives to Stripe, Square, Paypal, and other big tech payment processors.

**The 12 best alternatives to Stripe, Square, PayPal, and other big tech payment processors include:**

01. **Stax Payments**
02. **Ayden**
03. **WePay**
04. **Authorize.net**
05. **Braintree**
06. **2Checkout**
07. **Helcim**
08. **Payment Depot**
09. **Shopify**
10. **Clover**
11. **ChargeBee**
12. **Wise**

You’ll also discover why the best first step is to speak with a consultant who has relationships with many payment processing solution providers and can help you find the best overall solution, for free.

Let’s start with that.

## How a Consultant Can Help You Find the Best Payment Processing Solution for Free

Most business leaders and owners are unaware that they don’t have to spend endless hours researching to find the best payment processing solution for their business.

It’s possible to speak with a consultant who has relationships with many payment processing solution providers and can help you find the best overall solution, for free.

That’s right, working with a payments consultant costs your business nothing.

The consultant simply receives a small percentage of the margin generated through the payment processing solutions they help you with.

The solutions they recommend cost you nothing out of pocket, and the ongoing fees are usually significantly less than what businesses pay with Square, PayPal, or other big tech payment processors.

**A payments consultant can help you do the following, for free**:

**1) Get much better customer service for your payment processing needs.**

Often, when business leaders and owners encounter an issue with payment processing, they end up being routed through call centers in Asia to get help.

This is often very time-consuming, and frustrating.

A payments consultant can help you find a solution that includes easy access to a local representative, whom you can text or call for expert help fast.

**2) Significantly reduce costs related to payment processing.**

Most businesses are paying too much in fees for payment processing.

In almost all cases, a payments consultant can help businesses currently using Stripe, Square, PayPal, or other big tech payment processors dramatically reduce the costs associated with payment processing.

For example, businesses processing $5 million worth of sales per year typically save around $50,000 per year by working with a payments consultant.

That’s enough to hire another employee!

**3) Dramatically reduce fraud and chargebacks, and the time it takes to deal with those issues.**

Most businesses will invariably deal with fraud attempts and chargebacks.

When dealing with the big payment processing companies, these issues can be extremely costly in terms of both time and money.

A payments consultant can help you get the most advanced, up-to-date fraud protection.

A consultant can also help you highly customize the settings in your software to reduce both fraud and chargebacks.

Also, if an issue does arise, you’ll be able to text or call a local representative who can help you immediately.

**4) Essentially eliminate downtime for payment processing.**

Unfortunately, most payment processors have experienced significant downtime in the last few years because the payment gateways they use are not cloud-based.

Not being able to process payments can be very costly to a business and create a bad customer experience.

While they can’t guarantee that you’ll never experience downtime, a payments consultant can help ensure that your payment processing solution includes a cloud-based payment gateway that has experienced zero downtime in the last five years.

**5) Seamlessly integrate all your payments and accounting-related software.**

Many businesses end up using multiple solutions for payment processing that are unable to communicate with each other.

This results in extra, unnecessary costs, and lots of manual labor to produce meaningful reporting and reconcile accounts.

A payments consultant can help ensure that all components of your solution work together to save you and your employees time and money.

If you’d like to request a free consultation with a payments consultant in our network, just tap below to answer a few quick questions, and we’ll match you with a consultant who is the right fit for your business.

[**Request My Free Consultation**](https://businessleadershiptoday.com/survey-for-free-payments-consultation/)

## **What Is Stripe?**

Stripe is a prominent payment processing company, holding a 16% share of the global market and ranking just behind PayPal. While it serves a variety of businesses, it may not be the best fit for all small enterprises.

Stripe features a straightforward pricing structure, supports multiple payment methods, and allows for customization through its open Application Programming Interface (API). However, its complexity and costs may be prohibitive for some merchants, especially since it primarily caters to online transactions.

Although Stripe offers benefits like no fees for Payment Card Industry (PCI) compliance and flexible integration, there are notable drawbacks.

Accounts can be frozen due to suspected fraud, and there is no unified solution for processing offline and online payments. Its transaction fees can also be high for businesses with significant sales volume, and accessing customer support can be challenging.

There are alternatives available that provide better support and a more user-friendly experience, particularly for businesses that may lack technical expertise.

## **Why Choose an Alternative to Stripe?**

E-commerce, marketplaces, Software as a Service (SaaS), and high-volume businesses often explore alternatives to Stripe due to the intricacies involved in establishing a robust payment infrastructure, as a way to lower how much they spend in transaction fees, which can be costly for high-volume businesses.

When considering payment processing options, it’s important to evaluate alternatives to Stripe based on your transaction volume. These alternatives can often provide lower transaction fees, which means you’ll retain a larger portion of your earnings.

Some providers may offer better fraud prevention measures, safeguarding your business against potential losses due to fraudulent transactions.

Additionally, certain platforms take a more streamlined approach to handling charge disputes. This can save you time and resources, allowing you to focus on other important aspects of your business.

By reducing operating costs through these alternatives, you not only increase your profit margins but also open up opportunities to potentially increase your overall revenue.

While Stripe functions effectively as a payment gateway, it requires additional integrations to handle subscriptions, taxes, invoicing, and performance metrics, which can present challenges for companies that do not have a large team or developers on hand.

The inability to integrate payment methods like PayPal may complicate infrastructure development and increase the likelihood of encountering issues, potentially leading to a rise in support tickets and customer turnover.

Developers might find themselves dedicating time to maintaining existing tools or crafting their own solutions, as Stripe’s APIs, while enabling quicker setups, may require further efforts for optimization.

A highly customized payment stack can introduce complexities and slow down the response to growth opportunities.

Stripe’s tax liability considerations stem from the requirement to pay sales tax based on customer locations. Although the acquisition of TaxJar offers some benefits, users still bear the responsibility of managing their tax filings, as TaxJar’s automation is primarily focused on U.S. transactions.

Given these factors, some companies may find that they need to seek out more integrated solutions that better align with their customers and operational needs.

## **Choosing the Best Alternative to Stripe**

Choosing the right Stripe alternative can be challenging due to various factors that need to be considered.

### **Key Factors in Choosing a Stripe Alternative**

There are several important factors to consider when choosing a Stripe alternative.

Stripe’s high transaction fees, which are a standard fee for each transaction (e.g., 2.9% + $0.30 for most credit card payments in the U.S.), might be higher than competitors’ fees. They may also include hidden costs such as currency conversion fees that accumulate significantly.

**Payment Processing Cost:** Consider pricing models—Stripe, Square, and PayPal Zettle follow a flat-rate model, while interchange-plus providers pass on exact interchange fees, offering more transparency and potential savings.

**Contract Length:** Stripe’s absence of long-term contracts is a benefit. Always review terms and inquire about termination fees and contract lengths before choosing a service.

**POS Hardware Options:** Stripe’s focus is on online businesses, resulting in limited hardware offerings. Brick-and-mortar establishments might prefer providers with broader Point of Sales (POS) hardware selections.

**Merchant Account Options:** Stripe combines multiple businesses into one merchant account, which can lead to higher risks of freezes. Businesses requiring tailored solutions should explore individual merchant accounts, although the setup may take longer.

### **Steps to Make an Informed Decision**

It can be helpful to follow these guidelines when choosing an alternative:

**1**. **Evaluate Business Requirements:** Understand your needs, including product type, target audience, and transaction volume.

**2\. Integration and Compatibility:** Ensure smooth integration with your website or eCommerce platform.

**3.** **Scalability:** Choose a platform that accommodates business growth and increasing transactions.

**4\. Reputation:** Check the provider’s reputation, expertise, and client portfolio.

**5\. Customer Support:** Assess the quality and availability of customer support.

**6\. Cost and Fees:** Consider all costs, including hidden fees for features and currency conversion.

**7\. Trial and Evaluation:** Utilize free trials to assess the platform’s suitability.

### **Considerations When Choosing Alternatives**

For businesses looking to enhance features currently provided by Stripe but lacking in-house upgrade options, alternatives may offer better rates for accepting international payments, more affordable invoicing and billing management tools, and more established mobile apps supporting various payment methods.

For those seeking a fast setup and simple integrations, alternatives might provide out-of-the-box payment processing solutions instead of code-based setups, proprietary POS software pre-built into standalone terminals or as mobile apps, transaction fee discounts for qualified nonprofits, and ready integration with surcharging programs and platforms.

### **Evaluating Costs and Value**

After narrowing down your choices, it’s important to evaluate the estimated monthly fees associated with each competitor to Stripe.

Assess the overall value each provider offers, and if a provider charges for services that you deem unnecessary, consider exploring alternative options that may better meet your needs and budget.

### **Evaluating Hardware Features**

The physical devices used for credit card transactions are crucial. The hardware should support the payment methods most frequently used by your customers, such as chip and PIN cards, contactless cards, or mobile wallet payments like Apple Pay or Google Pay.

Depending on your business type, you may need robust, stationary terminals for a brick-and-mortar setup or portable devices for mobile operations. It’s important that the devices are user-friendly for both employees and customers and durable enough to withstand everyday use.

### **Evaluating Software Features**

Software is essential in a credit card processing system. It should integrate smoothly with your existing systems, such as accounting software or e-commerce platforms.

Security is essential; therefore, ensure that the software is compliant with PCI standards to protect against fraud and data breaches.

High-quality software should provide valuable reporting and analytics on sales trends, inventory, and other critical business data.

Additionally, comprehensive customer support is vital for effectively troubleshooting any potential issues.

### **Evaluating Credit Card Processing Fees**

Make sure you understand the fees associated with credit card processing. These typically include transaction fees, which are a percentage of each transaction and may include a fixed cost per transaction.

Some providers charge monthly or annual fees for access to their services. It’s also important to be aware of hidden fees, such as cancellation fees, compliance fees, or gateway fees, which can add up.

By considering these factors—hardware compatibility, software integration and security, and transparent fee structures—you can select a credit card processing system that meets the needs of your business and ensures smooth, efficient transactions.

### 1\. **Stax**

Stax, formerly Fatmerchant, is a payment processing platform that simplifies international payments for over 20,000 customers. With over $23 billion processed, it’s known for affordability.

#### **Key Features**

**Recurring Billing and Invoicing:** Includes powerful online invoicing features along with automatic customer reminders.

**Versatile Terminals:** Provides standalone terminals that seamlessly integrate with mobile and virtual terminal features.

#### **Limitations**

**Pro Plan:** $159 per month.

**Ultimate Plan:** $199 per month.

**Processing Fees:** An additional 8 cents per in-person transaction and 15 cents per manual entry.

**No Contracts:** No annual fees, batch fees, or markup on interchange rates.
